2025 Annual Report

  • 2025 In Review
  • Grant Highlights
  • People
  • Investments

2025 was a year of both/and, holding tensions and paradox while moving toward a hopeful vision of the future. 

It was a year of agility and challenge, of purpose and uncertainty, of honoring the past and leaning into the future. Across the social sector, from the national level down to our own neighborhoods, organizations navigated shifting funding landscapes, rising service demand, and the real weight of weariness. And yet we showed up as a sector for each other and those we serve. Leaders did the faithful work of staying focused on mission and community.  

Our engagement with each of you bolsters our belief in the potential of human flourishing for all, not just as a vision statement, but as a reality we can work towards together. 

2025 also marked the Murdock Trust’s 50th anniversary, and that milestone was its own kind of both-and. We paused to honor five decades of stewarding Jack Murdock’s resources for the common good, and we turned our eyes to what comes next. Listening sessions across the region gave us the gift of your candor and feedback, and our inaugural Jack Murdock Innovation Symposium brought us together to dream about what’s possible. The anniversary gave us permission to celebrate, and the moment gave us reasons to keep asking the right questions about the future. 

As I reflect on this past year, what fills me most is gratitude. Gratitude for a social sector that has always held the tension between the limits of any given moment and a sustaining vision of what our communities could become.  

We invite you to explore this report as a celebration of the fruitful work done by nonprofits across our region, and with gratitude for the places the Murdock Trust gets to intersect with it. Behind every inspiring story there is a whole set of questions and uncertainties that go unnamed but are just as much a part of the story we are all building together. 

Thank you for being part of ours. For the last fifty years, and for the next fifty together. 

A Look Back at 2025

$85m

Total Granted

337

Grants Awarded

1,500

Program Participants

$10.7m to Artistic & Cultural Expression

$33.7m to Civic Engagement & Community Services

$15.6m to Education & Leadership Development

$17.9m to Health & Environmental Stewardship

$6.7m to Scientific Research

50th Anniversary

2025 marked the Murdock Trust's 50th anniversary — a meaningful opportunity to reflect on the past and anticipate the future.

Three things guided our approach to this milestone: shining a light on the extraordinary organizations we've supported over five decades; giving every staff member a chance to connect directly with nonprofits; and honoring Jack Murdock, whose vision and legacy we carry forward every day.

To commemorate five decades of partnership, we visited each of the PNW states we serve, hosting receptions and listening sessions alongside nonprofit association conferences. We engaged with longtime grantees and new potential partners alike, celebrating their work and learning how we can best partner together in the years ahead.

This spirit of collaboration also inspired the inaugural Jack Murdock Innovation Symposium, bringing together hundreds of nonprofit leaders for a day of learning, networking, and bold thinking about what's next for the sector — a fitting tribute to a man whose belief in human potential and the power of community continues to animate everything we do.

We have always believed that the fruit of our labor grows on the trees of others. To all who have been part of our fifty years of history: thank you for all that you do to serve the common good!

Nonprofits at Work

In 2025, we saw nonprofits demonstrate incredible agility and creativity in the face of profound uncertainty and change to the nonprofit sector. The organizations below offer a glimpse of the Strategic Project grants the Murdock Trust was honored to support. Each one represents far more than a single project: it's a whole community investing its time, talent, and resources in what matters. View full list of Strategic Project grants here >

Interactive Nonprofits Gallery
Filters

Click/Tap to Learn More

Hold & Drag to See More

KTOO Public Media

Founded in 1972, KTOO Public Media serves as Southeast Alaska's trusted source for local news, arts, and civic engagement, reaching over 780,000 listeners and viewers annually across the state. A Trust grant will aid KTOO in upgrading its broadcast and streaming infrastructure to reduce latency, expand its digital reach, and ensure uninterrupted access to live programming even during power outages, when reliable communication matters most.

Covenant House Alaska

Covenant House Alaska provides emergency shelter, transitional housing, education, and wraparound services to youth experiencing homelessness across Alaska. With Trust support, the organization has hired a Chief Grants Strategy Officer to strengthen its development capacity, diversify its funding base, and expand its reach to youth in rural Alaska.

Arctic Slope Community Foundation

The Arctic Slope Community Foundation strengthens the eight remote communities of Alaska's Arctic Slope through grantmaking, education, and capacity-building programs. A Trust grant will support the Foundation to hire two new staff to launch a regional digital literacy initiative, equipping tribal organizations, municipalities, and community leaders with the technology skills needed to better serve their neighbors for generations to come.

Southcentral Foundation

Southcentral Foundation is an Alaska Native-owned healthcare organization serving over 2,500 employees and delivering more than 80 health and wellness programs to Native communities in Anchorage, the Mat-Su Borough, and 55 rural villages. With Trust support, Southcentral will complete construction of a 105,000 square foot behavioral health facility that will provide crisis stabilization, detox, medication-assisted treatment, and outpatient services, offering a critical alternative to emergency rooms for Alaskans in behavioral health crisis.

Prince William Sound Science & Technology Institute

For over 35 years, the Prince William Sound Science & Technology Institute has advanced scientific research, ecosystem monitoring, and education across coastal Alaska, forging partnerships with more than 100 organizations to build long-term resilience in the region. With Trust support, the Center will hire a Director of Coastal Resilience to lead a new interdisciplinary research program tackling climate-driven changes to Prince William Sound and beyond.

Friends of Zoo Boise

Zoo Boise is a conservation zoo serving over 19,800 students annually through hands-on wildlife education programs that inspire empathy and environmental stewardship. A Trust grant will support the construction of a new 9,000 square foot education facility with dedicated classrooms, an outdoor learning space, and full ADA accessibility, enabling the zoo to expand programming by 30% and serve thousands more students.

Idaho Botanical Gardens

Idaho Botanical Garden (IBG) is a community gathering space welcoming over 140,000 visitors annually through environmental education, cultural events, and programming — including donating over 4,300 pounds of fresh produce to its community last year. With Trust support, IBG will develop 10 uncultivated acres with new gardens, universally accessible walkways, and an outdoor amphitheater, laying the groundwork for 40,000 additional visitors, expanded food security programming, and community events.

Boys & Girls Clubs of Magic Valley

The Boys and Girls Club of Magic Valley serves youth across 10 sites in South Central Idaho, providing mentorship, meals, and programming for just $20 a year in communities where poverty and food insecurity are persistent challenges. A Trust grant will support the Rupert Club in renovating a new children's clubhouse, enabling the organization to more than double its daily capacity and create age-appropriate spaces for youth.

Special Olympics Idaho

Special Olympics Idaho (SOI) empowers people with intellectual disabilities through inclusive sports, health screenings, and school-based programs that build confidence, community, and belonging across the state. With Trust support, SOI will hire staff to expand programming into rural and Tribal communities, bringing the transformative benefits of sport and free health screenings to Idahoans who have long faced barriers to access.

Fort Peck Fine Arts Council

Fort Peck Fine Arts Council stewards the Historic Fort Peck Theatre, bringing performing arts and community events to all ages in Montana's Fort Peck community. With Trust support, the Council will renovate the theatre facility, expanding its capacity to host performances, educational programming, and community gatherings that strengthen cultural vitality and connection across the region.

Mountain Shadow Association

Mountain Shadow Association is a community-based, Native-American nonprofit located in Bozeman, Montana, aiding families on the path to recovery and providing resources that advance sobriety and healing. A Trust grant for the construction of Children’s House will support children of adults going through substance abuse treatment and offer residential space, culturally specific caregiving, and educational support.

Code Girls United

Code Girls United brings coding, technology, AI, and workforce readiness to 1,848 girls across rural and tribal communities in Montana, addressing the critical shortage of women and Native Americans in tech careers. A Trust grant will support new program staff dedicated to impact evaluation and program rigor, enabling the organization to strengthen the validity of its offerings, measure outcomes across its 39 programs, and expand pathways to tech careers and scholarships for underrepresented girls in Montana.

Piikanii Lodge Health Institute

Piikanii Lodge Health Institute is an Indigenous-founded and led nonprofit dedicated to promoting the health and well-being of Blackfeet people and lands through regenerative practices rooted in Blackfeet traditional knowledge and culture. A Trust grant will support new program staff focused on reducing climate-related health risks and improving wellness outcomes for Blackfeet Nation community members, enabling the organization to deepen its impact across health and wellbeing, food sovereignty, economic development, and environmental stewardship initiatives grounded in Blackfeet ways of being and land stewardship.

Montana State University

Montana State University (MSU) is the state's largest university and a Carnegie top-tier research institution, increasing discovery across STEM and beyond. The Trust is supporting MSU’s research through a grant for Markerless Motion Capture Equipment, which will advance research in neuromuscular biomechanics and foster a deeper understanding of human movement.

The John G. Shedd Institute for the Arts

The John G. Shedd Institute for the Arts, founded in the early 1990s, is a nonprofit performing arts venue, educational institute, and community music school with year-round professional performances, educational programs, and performances representing the rich diversity of American music. Through a Trust facility renovation grant, the Institute will broaden access to arts education and performing arts for all ages, continuing its goal of being a cultural resource serving Eugene, Lane County, and all of Oregon.

Our Just Future

Our Just Future has supported homeless and low-income families residing in outer East Portland and East Multnomah County since 1988. A Trust grant will fund a new Deputy Director position to strengthen and expand housing and associated support services for people with low income, advancing the organization's vision of vibrant neighborhoods where all people share in the security and advantages of a thriving community.

Northeast Oregon Area Health Education Center

Founded in 1991, Northeast Oregon Area Health Education Center works to address the healthcare workforce shortage in rural Oregon by developing health professionals that reflect the composition and needs of the communities they serve. A Trust grant will fund new program staff to strengthen health career pathway programs, increasing the reach of career exploration and educational opportunities for students across hard-to-reach rural regions and building a sustainable healthcare workforce for Eastern Oregon.

Wild Salmon Center

For over three decades, Wild Salmon Center (WSC) has protected wild salmon across the North Pacific, spanning Russia, Japan, Alaska, British Columbia, and the Pacific Northwest. WSC's mission is to promote the conservation and sustainable use of wild salmon ecosystems across the Pacific Rim by identifying science-based, pragmatic solutions that sustain wild salmonids and the human communities and livelihoods that depend on them. A Trust grant will further these solutions by funding program staff to advance sustainable fisheries reform.

University of Oregon

Located in Eugene, Oregon, University of Oregon is a public research university advancing discovery and innovation for cutting-edge research. A Trust grant will support the acquisition of a D8 Discover X-Ray Diffractometer to replace and upgrade x-ray materials analysis capabilities in a manner that will increase internal (academia) and external (academia and industry) users in the region, focusing on transformative research in novel and sustainable energy. 

Oregon State University

Oregon State University is a public research university in Corvallis, Oregon, with a focus on advanced manufacturing research and metal additive manufacturing. Trust support will help purchase a composition measurement system platform for the university's metal additive manufacturing laboratory, strengthening advanced metal and additive manufacturing research and capabilities across the Pacific Northwest.

Yəhaw̓ Indigenous Creatives Collective

Indigenous Creatives Collective is an urban, Native, women-led arts nonprofit rematriating 2 acres of land and amplifying Indigenous creativity across Coast Salish territories in the Pacific Northwest. The organization offers radically inclusive and equitable arts opportunities, including exhibitions, grants, cultural workshops, an artist roster, and land restoration activities. A Trust grant will support the construction of a new facility, enabling the organization to connect audiences to expanded programming centered on Indigenous land rematriation, art-making, wellness, and cultural practices.

Harvest Against Hunger

Harvest Against Hunger connects farmers, truckers, volunteers, and communities across Washington to reduce hunger and food waste by moving healthy produce from farms and fields to those in need. Through programs including Farm to Community partnerships with small-scale growers, produce donations, trucking logistics, and the Harvest VISTA AmeriCorps program, the organization builds sustainable connections between agricultural partners and local hunger relief organizations. A Trust grant will enable the hiring of new communications staff to increase health outcomes through awareness of and access to healthy food.

Black Pilots of America

Black Pilots of America empowers underrepresented youth across the nation to pursue aviation careers through hands-on flight training, education, and mentorship. A Trust grant will provide equipment and technology to strengthen STEM and aviation instruction in BPA's Summer Flight Academy and chapter programs, enabling the organization to expand access and deepen learning opportunities for the next generation of student pilots.

Washington State Opportunity Scholarship

Washington State Opportunity Scholarship (WSOS) builds pathways to post-secondary education and high-demand careers for students across Washington through financial assistance, mentorship, and career development. A Trust grant will enable WSOS to hire new program staff dedicated to building an alumni network that connects graduates to employment opportunities and career advancement, strengthening workforce development across high-need sectors and advancing economic opportunity throughout Washington communities.

Free Clinic of Southwest Washington

Free Clinic of Southwest Washington provides free, compassionate health care and health navigation services to uninsured and underinsured community members in Southwest Washington. With Trust support, the Free Clinic will make leasehold improvements that expand its clinical capacity and services, strengthening its ability to reach more patients and improve health outcomes across the community.

Benaroya Research Institute

Benaroya Research Institute is an independent biomedical research institute in Seattle, Washington, dedicated to advancing research on diseases of the immune system. The institute's scientists conduct fundamental and translational immunology research through four Centers of Discovery and specialized core laboratories. Trust support will help purchase a fluorescence-activated cell sorter (FACS) instrument, enabling expanded biomedical research capacity in the Human Immunophenotyping Core to advance the institute's mission to predict, prevent, reverse and cure diseases of the immune system.

Prison Fellowship

Prison Fellowship Ministries is the nation's largest Christian nonprofit serving incarcerated individuals, reaching over 550,000 people and their families annually through programs that address spiritual, emotional, educational, and reentry needs. A Trust grant will support a new digital tablet platform to deliver tools to incarcerated individuals across the Pacific Northwest and beyond.

Praxis

Founded in 2012, Praxis partners with innovators, founders, and entrepreneurs to support mission-aligned investment through programming. A Trust grant will expand Praxis's programming capacity to engage with contemporary issues through the development of redemptive ventures that create tangible social impact.

Trout Unlimited

Trout Unlimited works across private, public, and Tribal lands to restore coldwater ecosystems and recover wild trout and salmon populations. A Trust grant will equip this national nonprofit to hire a Pacific Northwest Stewardship Officer to manage grants, ensure compliance, and provide administrative capacity to partner organizations, freeing conservation staff to focus on on-the-ground habitat restoration across PNW watersheds.

Trinity Western University

Trinity Western University (TWU) is a private liberal arts university in Langley, British Columbia (BC), serving approximately 7,000 students from around the world. In response to Canada’s growing nursing shortage, TWU is undergoing a multi-stage upscaling of their School of Nursing. Trust support will help purchase learning and technology equipment for the first phase of this program expansion.

Programming

The Murdock Trust welcomed more than 1,500 nonprofit leaders and staff into conferences, convenings, and cohort-style programs in 2025 designed to spark the questions that advanced sustainability and innovation: Where are we? Who else is doing this? What's next? From the inaugural Jack Murdock Innovation Symposium to milestones in some of our longest-running programs, it was a year of remarkable learning, collaboration, and creativity. 

Cohort-Style Programs

Board Leadership

In 2025, 190 individuals across 33 organizations deepened their board leadership skills through this year-long, cohort-style program with customized coaching. 

Essentials of Development

78 participants from 22 organizations sharpened their fundraising and development strategies through a blend of in-person gatherings, virtual webinars, and customized coaching.

Green Room: Board Leadership for Leaders of Color

30 leaders of color came together in our 2025 cohort to build skills and community, equipping them to deepen existing board commitments or step into board leadership for the first time.

Peer Cohorts

2025 saw the second year of Excelling Beyond the Beginning, supporting 12 executive directors in years three through six of their leadership, and the inaugural year of our Leaders of Color cohort, welcoming 12 executive directors of color.

Conferences & Convenings

Leadership Now

238 executive directors of faith-based nonprofits, plus Trust staff and partners, gathered for two days of learning, community, and encouragement, exploring the topic "Co-Creating the Future: How Nonprofits Grow Gardens in Challenging Landscapes” together.

Vision & Call Retreat

112 interns joined 61 advisers and mentors in Yelm, WA for a weekend of connection, learning, and vocational discernment at our annual Vision & Call retreat.

Murdock College Science Research Conference

Now in its 34th year, the MCSR conference brought together 330 students and 90 faculty from 31 institutions for two days of presentations, peer networking, and collaborative learning.

Partners in Science Research Symposium

The research conference for our Partners in Science program welcomed nearly 100 science educators (high school science teachers and university mentors), hosted for the first year in Portland, OR. 

Partners in Science Teaching Practices Conference

26 first-year and 13 second-year Partners came together in August for a hands-on day of learning, idea exchange, and peer connection.

Jack Murdock Innovation Symposium

In celebration of the Murdock Trust's 50th anniversary, more than 300 nonprofit leaders and staff, plus Trust staff and partners, from across the region convened for keynotes, breakout sessions, and meaningful conversation about the future of innovation in the nonprofit sector.

Welcoming New Faces to the Trust

Press Enter or Space to view biography. Press Escape to close.

Aaron Putzke joined as Vice President for Scientific Research, leading this focus area’s grantmaking and programming to use scientific research for the common good. 

We welcomed Ashley Castino as our Cloud Applications Administrator, applying her expertise to evolving our web-based platforms to meet business needs.

We welcomed Jeremy Likes as our new Investment Associate, providing reporting, modeling, and analytical support for existing investments and recommendations.

Valentina Chau joined as our Event & Hospitality Planner, ensuring all logistics of Murdock Trust events, meetings, and convenings are effectively executed.

Dr. Moses Lee

After 12 years at the Murdock Trust, Dr. Moses Lee retired from his role as Vice President of Scientific Research Grants & Programming in July 2025. Starting as a Program Director in 2013, Dr. Lee brought a rare combination of deep scientific expertise and visionary leadership to his role, overseeing nearly 600 grants and evolving science programming as the needs of the region changed. Just as impactful, his joyful presence, vibrant energy, and genuine passion for people and science shaped our team and the broader scientific community in ways we will always cherish. 

"I have worked with few individuals more passionate, energetic, and genuinely knowledgeable about their work than Dr. Moses Lee. It is in this spirit that Moses truly embodies the legacy of our benefactor, Jack Murdock. We are deeply grateful for his dedicated service." — Romanita Hairston, CEO, Murdock Trust 

Total Assets, Grants Approved & Grants Paid

In 2025, we approved more than $85 million in new grants and processed grant payments of more than $97 million in commitments. In a year marked by significant uncertainty across the broader landscape, disciplined fiscal management remained essential to fulfilling our mission, ensuring that every dollar was stewarded with care and directed toward meaningful impact. Hover over the graph below to learn more about the Trust's history of giving.

Hover over the graph for more information. (Amounts in thousands. Example: $1,140,574 = $1,140,574,000)

Investments

The Murdock Trust kept a steady hand on our long-term investment strategy while staying responsive to a year of notable market complexity in 2025. We continued realigning allocations where needed, always in close partnership with our trusted managers. Our team remains committed, as ever, to stewarding these resources well in support of nonprofits and the communities they serve. 

The Murdock Trust divides all potential investments into either low, medium, or high “risk/reward buckets” and considers all types of investment products — without preconceived ideas or built-in biases — for all three categories. Our investment objectives follow directly from this “three-bucket” allocation strategy. More information on this strategy ›

Investment Types

Low Risk/Return
Medium Risk/Return
High Risk/Return

About Our Benefactor

Our benefactor, Melvin J. “Jack” Murdock, was an avid learner, innovator, and entrepreneur with business interests throughout the Pacific Northwest. At a young age, he established his own business selling and servicing radios and electrical appliances, and from there he began working with Howard Vollum. The two would go on to develop the oscilloscope and co-found Tektronix, which would become a major Oregon employer and contribute significantly to the tech growth of the Pacific Northwest. Upon his untimely death in 1971, his will directed three Trustees to establish a charitable trust that would support thriving communities in the region he lived in and loved.

Learn About Jack › View Jack’s Timeline ›

Our Mission

To build capacity in nonprofits driving innovative and sustainable outcomes in the Pacific Northwest.

Our Vision and Values ›
